  # IWW CD09-13 DNA ELEMENTS â€” Dan Kennedy
  # Influential Writing Workshop (CDs 9 through 13)

  # ============================================================

  # --- IWW CD09-13 L1_PHILOSOPHIES ---

  - id: "FIL-DK-669"
    title: "Everything Done By Deliberate Strategy"
    content: >
      Everything about your business communications should be deliberate.
      It appears that you do everything deliberately â€” and they are right.
      It is not particularly smart to be accidental. Most people just sit
      down and write newsletters without strategy, and that is not the way
      to get results.
    source: "IWW-CD09-T01"
    domain: "marketing"

  - id: "FIL-DK-670"
    title: "All Secrets Are Visible But Many Are Unseen"
    content: >
      All of our secrets are visible, but many are unseen. People see
      what we do but they do not attach any importance to it. Like Ernie
      Kessler handling a glass of water at exactly the same moment on
      exactly the same word every single time â€” people see it but do not
      realize it is deliberate.
    source: "IWW-CD09-T01"
    domain: "influence"

  - id: "FIL-DK-671"
    title: "Typography Is The Dress We Put On Copy"
    content: >
      Typography is the dress we put on the copy. Fonts communicate in
      and of themselves. If I show you a typeface and do not let you read
      the words, there is still a certain level of communication. Typewriter
      look says you sat down and did it yourself. Helvetica screams
      commercially prepared. Script conveys femininity or emotional topics.
    source: "IWW-CD09-T01"
    domain: "copywriting"

  - id: "FIL-DK-672"
    title

  - id: "FIL-DK-681"
    title: "Seminars must become UNIQUE EXPERIENCES â€” the seminar-as-information-delivery model is dead"
    content: >
      Moving from a seminar to a unique experience is the secret sauce. These are no longer
      seminars. They really have to be unique experiences. Networking events, special meal
      functions, book signings, meet and greets, contests, award ceremonies, VIP lounges â€”
      all create an experience people cannot get from a recording.

    title: "The person who cannot afford it WILL come â€” never assume the broke prospect won't buy"

      in this room there are at least a few who absolutely cannot afford to be here but they're
      here anyway. They had to run it on eight credit cards, borrow from their mother-in-law,
      sell something. Don't ever think they're not in your herd.\

## Overview

"Butt In Seats and Bucks in the Bank" is a comprehensive program on seminar/event marketing delivered by Dan Kennedy (psychology of attendance) and Bill Glazer (tactical execution, logistics, choreography). Presented at a Glazer-Kennedy Info Summit with nearly 50 years of collective experience and over $100M generated from seminars attended by over 1 million people.

## Content Structure

| File | Speaker | Topic | Size |
|------|---------|-------|------|
| CD 1 | Bill Glazer | 7 Chief Factors + 39 Marketing Boosters (Part 1) | 39K |

| DVD 1 | Dan Kennedy | Psychology of Seminar Attendance | 71K |
| DVD 2 | Bill Glazer | Extended logistics + Frank Kern + Pre-registration | 84K |
| Exhibits Manual | Both | Slide deck/visual exhibits (147 pages, mostly images) | 9K |
| Full Transcript | Both | Official written transcript (60 pages) | 145K |

| Domain | Elements | Key Content |

| event-marketing | 35 | Complete event marketing system |
| attendance-psychology | 10 | Buyer motivation spectrum, why people attend/don't |
| marketing-funnels | 15 | 90+ touches, diverse media, repurposing |
| event-logistics | 12 | Site selection, contract negotiation, vendor tips |
| event-choreography | 8 | Profit maximization, speaker position

## QUEM SOU EU

I am Eugene M. Schwartz. I wrote Breakthrough Advertising. I wrote more winning ads than most copywriters will ever read. For forty years I sat at my desk and studied what makes people act â€” not what makes them think, not what makes them feel good about your brand â€” what makes them reach for their wallet, fill out the coupon, pick up the phone, and BUY.

I am not a creative writer. I am an engineer of desire. There is no such thing as "creating" desire. Desire already exists in the mass market â€” millions of people wanting health, wealth, beauty, status, security, pleasure. My job â€” the copywriter's ONLY job â€” is to take that existing desire and CHANNEL it onto your product. ^[FIL-ES-001]

I studied physics and mathematics before I studied advertising. And I brought that precision with me. Advertising is not art. It is applied psychology with measurable results. Every headline can be tested. Every claim can be measured. Every word either pulls the reader forward or loses him. There is no middle ground. ^[FIL-ES-002]

I worked in 33-minute sessions. Not because I lacked endurance, but because the unconscious mind requires pressure followed by release. You push intensely for 33 minutes, then you stop â€” completely. You do something utterly unrelated. And when you return, the connections have been made for you, beneath the surface. This is not mysticism. This is how the brain works. ^[HEUR-ES-001]

## Overview

Breakthrough Advertising is widely regarded as the single most important book ever written on direct response copywriting and advertising strategy. Eugene Schwartz wrote it in 1966 and it has remained continuously influential for over 60 years. Physical copies have sold for $500-$3,000+ on the secondary market. The book's central thesis is revolutionary: **you cannot create desire â€” you can only channel, direct, and focus existing desire that already lives in the mass market.** Schwartz argues that the copywriter's job is not persuasion but identification and redirection of forces that already exist.

The book covers three macro-systems: (1) the 5 Levels of Market Awareness that determine how you open your ad, (2) the 5 Stages of Market Sophistication that determine how you position your claim, and (3) a battery of intensification techniques that strengthen desire, belief, and identification throughout the body of the ad.

---

## Overview

The Brilliance Breakthrough is Eugene Schwartz's second and final book, published 28 years after Breakthrough Advertising. While BA focuses on market strategy and ad construction, BB focuses on the micro-mechanics of language â€” how to make every sentence vivid, memorable, and impossible to ignore. The book's core thesis: **brilliance is not a gift â€” it is a learnable technique of simplification, picture-making, and connecting.** Schwartz argues that all great communication reduces complex ideas to images that a five-year-old could understand, then connects those images with emotional pivots.

Where Breakthrough Advertising tells you WHAT to say and WHEN to say it (awareness + sophistication), The Brilliance Breakthrough tells you HOW to say it at the sentence level. The two books form a complete system: strategy (BA) + execution (BB).

The book introduces the Picture Word / Connecting Word dichotomy, the Simplification Procedure, the Pivot Word System, 2 Search Commands for finding stories, and 12 Implication Devices that let you say more by saying less.

**MM-TB-203**
- **name:** Front-End / Back-End Profit Architecture
- **description:** The front-end funnel is a customer acquisition machine designed to break even â€” the back-end is where all real profit is extracted from the same customers over time. These two phases have completely different goals.

**MM-TB-204**

- **description:** Every element of your funnel that beats the current version becomes the new "control" â€” the baseline everything else is tested against. Marketing improvement is a scientific, iterative loop, not a one-time design.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** testing, optimization, direct-response

**MM-TB-205**
- **name:** Focus on Things That Scream, Not Whisper
- **description:** Testing resources should be concentrated on the elements with the highest leverage potential â€” offer, headline, big idea â€” not on minor cosmetic elements like button color or background color that can only whisper small improvements.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** testing, prioritization, optimization

**MM-TB-206**
- **name:** Lifetime Customer Value as Growth Accelerator
- **description:** Knowing LCV unlocks the ability to go intentionally negative on the front-end, spending more to acquire customers than they return initially, because the long-term customer value justifies the short-term loss.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** metrics, backend, growth

**MM-TB-207**
- **name:** Campaign Funnel as Sequential Revenue Map
- **description:** Each ad campaign must track the entire customer journey â€” from click to opt-in to front-end sale to upsell/add-on â€” with each step's revenue attributed back to the originating campaign to calculate true ROI.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** tracking, funnel, attribution

**MM-TB-208**
- **name:** Optimization by Constraint Identification

- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** optimization, funnel, tracking

**MM-TB-209**
- **name:** Customer Segmentation by Behavior Value

- **source_file:** Module 12 / transcription_mp4_module12-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** segmentation, backend, customer-value

**MM-TB-210**
- **name:** Format Laddering â€” Same Content, Higher Price Points

## L3 â€” HEURISTICS (Practical Rules, Thresholds, Numbers)

**HEUR-TB-200**
- **name:** 100% ROI = Break-Even, Not Profit
- **description:** An ROI of exactly 100% means the campaign cost was returned with zero profit generated. Anything above 100% is profit; below 100% is a loss. This calculation: ROI = (Sales Value / Campaign Cost) Ã— 100.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson3.txt | transcription_pdf_Module9-Lesson_3.txt
- **domains:** metrics, roi, tracking

**HEUR-TB-201**
- **name:** Every Ad and Channel Must Be Tracked Separately
- **description:** No two ads or traffic channels should share the same tracking link. Each individual ad and each individual channel must have its own campaign so performance can be evaluated independently.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** tracking, paid-traffic, attribution

**HEUR-TB-202**
- **name:** Track Clicks as Once Per Session (Not Always)
- **description:** When setting up tracking campaigns, count clicks as "once per session" to avoid skewing opt-in rates and other metrics. Counting every click inflates numbers and makes data unreliable.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** tracking, setup, data-integrity

**HEUR-TB-203**
- **name:** Change Sales Count to Zero for Add-On Offer Tracking Codes
- **description:** When adding revenue tracking code to upsell/add-on pages, change the "sales=1" parameter to "sales=0" to avoid counting a single buyer as multiple sales â€” preserving accurate sale count while still capturing add-on revenue.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** tracking, setup, upsells

**HEUR-TB-204**
- **name:** If CPS Equals AFEV â€” Keep Running the Campaign
- **description:** When cost per sale equals average front-end transaction value (break-even), the campaign should continue running. Break-even customer acquisition is perfectly acceptable and desirable given back-end value.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson2.txt | transcription_pdf_Module10-Lesson_2.txt
- **domains:** metrics, decision-making, paid-traffic

**HEUR-TB-205**
- **name:** If CPC Exceeds EPC â€” Fix Before Scaling
- **description:** When cost per click is higher than earnings per click, every additional click loses money. The remedy is to (1) lower ad costs, (2) increase sales conversion rate, and/or (3) increase average front-end transaction value.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** metrics, decision-making, optimization

**HEUR-TB-206**
- **name:** If LCV Is Not Increasing â€” Create More Offers
- **description:** Stagnant or flat lifetime customer value is a reliable indicator that not enough new offers are being made to existing customers frequently enough. The fix is always more prolific back-end offer creation.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** metrics, backend, customer-value

**HEUR-TB-207**
- **name:** Do Not Cancel a Campaign That Allows Break-Even Acquisition
- **description:** Any campaign where the customer is acquired at break-even â€” no matter how high the CPC â€” should not be cancelled. It continues to add customers to the back-end pipeline at zero net cost.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** paid-traffic, decision-making, metrics

**HEUR-TB-208**
- **name:** Test One Element at a Time
- **description:** AB split testing should change exactly one element per test (offer, OR headline, OR big idea). Testing multiple elements simultaneously makes it impossible to attribute which variable caused the result.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** testing, split-testing, methodology

**HEUR-TB-209**
- **name:** Test in Priority Order: Offer â†’ Headline â†’ Big Idea
- **description:** Testing should begin with the offer (highest leverage), then the headline/hook, then the big idea/premise. Testing lower-leverage elements first (button color, background) is a misuse of testing resources.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson2.txt | transcription_pdf_Module11-Lesson_2.txt
- **domains:** testing, prioritization, optimization

**HEUR-TB-210**
- **name:** Offer Changes Can Double Response; Button Color Cannot
- **description:** Changing the offer or its positioning has the potential to double conversion rates. Cosmetic changes (button color, background color) do not. This guides the prioritization of what to test.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** testing, offer-creation, conversion

**HEUR-TB-211**
- **name:** Visual Website Optimizer Entry Price ~$40/month (Free Trial Available)
- **description:** Visual Website Optimizer is a paid split testing tool available for approximately $40/month, with a free trial and a reduced entry-level tier (~$19/month) available after trial expiration for more advanced testing than Google Analytics provides.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson4.txt
- **domains:** tools, testing, budget

**HEUR-TB-212**
- **name:** Multivariate Testing Requires Significantly More Traffic
- **description:** Multivariate testing (testing multiple elements simultaneously) requires dramatically more traffic to reach statistical significance than simple AB split tests. Not recommended for low-traffic funnels in early stages.
- **source_file:** Module 11 / transcription_mp4_module11-lesson4.txt
- **domains:** testing, traffic, methodology

**HEUR-TB-213**
- **name:** AFEV Tells You the Maximum You Can Spend Per Customer
- **description:** Average front-end transaction value sets the ceiling for safe customer acquisition cost. Spending more than AFEV per sale means going negative on the front-end, which is only acceptable with a proven, profitable back-end.
- **source_file:** Module 10 / transcription_mp4_module10-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** metrics, unit-economics, customer-acquisition

**HEUR-TB-214**
- **name:** Gross Profit Formula: Sales Value Minus Campaign Cost
- **description:** Gross profit from any campaign is calculated as total sales value minus total campaign cost. HyperTracker labels this "gross profit" because it excludes physical product costs and shipping.
- **source_file:** Module 9 / transcription_mp4_module9-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** metrics, tracking, profitability

**HEUR-TB-215**
- **name:** Format Price Ladder: $19 â†’ $97 â†’ $397 â†’ $997 â†’ $3,000+
- **description:** The same core content can be productized at escalating price points by format: book (~$19), CDs (~$97), DVDs+manuals (~$397), live online coaching with community (~$997), live weekend event (~$3,000+).

- **Module 11 (Testing):** The data-driven testing philosophy, the three-priority testing hierarchy (Offer â†’ Headline â†’ Big Idea), one-element-at-a-time AB testing, Google Analytics Experiments, and Visual Website Optimizer.
- **Module 12 (Backend):** The core belief that profit lives in the back-end, prolific offer creation, format laddering ($19â€“$3,000+), four backend product categories, and customer segmentation by value behavior.</result>

---

**FIL-TB-100**
- **name:** Front-End Is Customer Acquisition Investment, Not Expense
- **description:** The biggest players in direct response marketing don't view their front-end marketing as an expense â€” they view it as an investment in customer acquisition, future profits, and business growth. The entire sequence from lead to thank-you page is the "front-end" and should be treated as an investment vehicle.

**FIL-TB-101**

- **description:** The objective of the front-end funnel is NOT to generate profit â€” it is to acquire the maximum number of new customers at break-even. Profit lives in the back-end. This philosophy unlocks the ability to outspend all competitors for new customers.

**FIL-TB-102**
- **name:** The Marketer Who Can Spend the Most to Acquire a Customer Wins
- **description:** All else being equal, the marketer who can invest the most money to acquire a single new customer wins the market. Second-chance offers, transaction maximization sequences, and back-end profits all exist to raise this ceiling and dominate competitors.

**FIL-TB-103**
- **name:** Consumption Drives Repeat Purchases
- **description:** Customers only reap benefits from information products when they actually consume them. If a customer buys and doesn't consume, the likelihood of buying another product decreases significantly. Driving consumption is not just service â€” it is a prerequisite for repeat sales.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** customer-success, email-marketing, info-products, retention

---

**FIL-TB-104**
- **name:** Upsells Should Never Imply the Initial Offer Is Incomplete
- **description:** Any add-on offer that implies the customer captivation offer is incomplete will destroy trust and anger new customers. Every add-on must stand as an enhancer â€” it either improves results or provides the next step, without signaling that the base product falls short.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 2, 3, 4 (mp4)
- **domains:** marketing-funnels, offer-design, copywriting, customer-trust

---

**FIL-TB-105**
- **name:** The Order Form Must Stand Alone as a Sales Piece
- **description:** The ideal order form should be compelling enough to generate a sale on its own, even if a prospect reached it without consuming any prior marketing. This requires affirmation copy, offer recap, testimonials, and guarantees â€” not just payment fields.
- **source_file:** Module 5 - Lesson 1 (mp4)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, copywriting, offer-design

---

**FIL-TB-106**
- **name:** Second-Chance Offers Are Leaving Nothing on the Table
- **description:** Presenting add-on offers only once during the initial ordering process wastes a tremendous amount of potential revenue. Every skipped offer is a second-chance opportunity â€” prospects often need to experience the initial product before investing more, and withholding that opportunity is a self-imposed revenue ceiling.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 2 (mp4)
- **domains:** email-marketing, marketing-funnels, revenue-optimization

## L2 â€” MENTAL MODELS (MM-TB-NNN)

---

**MM-TB-100**
- **name:** Transaction Value as Competitive Moat
- **description:** Front-end transaction value is the moat that determines which traffic channels and media buys you can access. A higher average front-end value per customer means you can use channels your competitors cannot afford â€” this is an asymmetric competitive advantage created entirely by funnel structure.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 1 (mp4)
- **domains:** marketing-funnels, competitive-strategy, paid-traffic

---

**MM-TB-101**
- **name:** Consumer Add-On Psychology (Real World Commerce Model)
- **description:** In real-world commerce, add-on offers at the point of purchase are always a fraction of the original item's price â€” never more. McDonald's super-size is less than the meal; a tie is less than a $2,000 suit. When you price upsells above the original offer, you violate consumer expectations and create friction. When you follow this pattern, you tap into an existing psychological reflex.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 2 (mp4)
- **domains:** consumer-psychology, offer-design, pricing

---

**MM-TB-102**
- **name:** Chain Sequence as Behavioral Funnel
- **description:** Unlike linear autoresponder sequences, the chain sequence is entirely behavior-driven. Customers are subscribed and unsubscribed to lists based solely on what they purchase. This creates a dynamic follow-up system that is always relevant to where the customer actually is, not where you assumed they would be.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 3 (mp4)
- **domains:** email-marketing, marketing-automation, behavioral-targeting

---

**MM-TB-103**
- **name:** Transformation Story Three-Phase Structure
- **description:** The most effective customer transformation stories have three phases: (1) the protagonist starts in the same or worse situation than the prospect, triggering "if they can do it, I can too"; (2) the discovery moment â€” finding your solution; (3) the transformation â€” the life the prospect wants to live. This structure works without explicit claims because it delivers the message through narrative.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** copywriting, storytelling, email-marketing

---

**MM-TB-104**
- **name:** Problem-Agitate-Solve (PAS) Email Framework
- **description:** A core email copywriting mental model: remind the prospect of their problem, agitate by showing the consequences of inaction or wrong solutions, then present your offer as the perfect solution. The agitation phase is critical â€” it creates urgency by making the cost of not acting viscerally clear.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** copywriting, email-marketing

---

**MM-TB-105**
- **name:** Progress Bar as Transaction Psychology Tool
- **description:** A checkout progress status bar conveys to customers that they are still in the middle of a transaction â€” not done yet. This keeps them engaged with add-on offers. Critically, the progress bar on the order form should NOT show "product options" (future upsells), as that signals the purchase is incomplete and creates friction on the front end.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 5 (mp4)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, consumer-psychology, funnel-design

---

**MM-TB-106**
- **name:** Frankly Puzzled Email â€” Objection Reframe at End of Sequence
- **description:** The final email in a prospect follow-up sequence should name the elephant in the room: "You've seen all this, and still haven't bought â€” it must be one of these three reasons." Then overcome the three most common objections. This reframe is more powerful than another benefit email because it acknowledges resistance directly and dissolves it.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** copywriting, email-marketing, objection-handling

---

**MM-TB-107**
- **name:** The Soft Offer as Trust Bridge
- **description:** The soft add-on (pay nothing now, billed in 72 hours) functions as a trust bridge between the initial transaction and a higher-priced upgrade. By acknowledging that the customer hasn't yet experienced the product, and offering value with zero immediate risk, it converts at dramatically higher rates than a conventional upsell at this stage.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 3 (mp4)
- **domains:** offer-design, consumer-psychology, marketing-funnels

---

**MM-TB-108**
- **name:** Thank You Page as Psychological Reset
- **description:** After a sequence of upsell pages, a mock "Thank You page" resets the customer's psychological state. When they feel the transaction is over and let their guard down, a gift offer presented as appreciation â€” not a sale â€” achieves dramatically higher take rates than continued upsell framing.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** consumer-psychology, offer-design, marketing-funnels

---

## L3 â€” HEURISTICS (HEUR-TB-NNN)

---

**HEUR-TB-100**
- **name:** Reverse Add-On Priced Up to 80% of Customer Captivation Offer
- **description:** The reverse add-on should never exceed 80% of the price of the customer captivation offer. This mirrors real-world consumer add-on psychology where upgrades are always a fraction of the original purchase. The example given: $297 CCO â†’ $199 reverse add-on (67%).
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 2 and pdf (Lesson 2)
- **domains:** pricing, offer-design, marketing-funnels

---

**HEUR-TB-101**
- **name:** Reverse Add-On Conversion Rate: 50â€“62%+
- **description:** A well-structured reverse add-on that is congruent, adds value, and is priced correctly should convert at over 50%. Todd reports one reverse add-on running at 62%+ consistently for four months. Benchmarks below 50% indicate a congruency, pricing, or copy issue.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 3 (mp4)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, marketing-funnels, performance-benchmarks

---

**HEUR-TB-102**
- **name:** Soft Add-On Priced Between $67 and $97
- **description:** The soft add-on (pay-nothing-now, 72-hour billed) should be priced between $67 and $97. This range is low enough to feel risk-free when the billing is deferred and the customer hasn't yet consumed the initial product.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 3 and pdf (Lesson 3)
- **domains:** pricing, offer-design, marketing-funnels

---

**HEUR-TB-103**
- **name:** Soft Add-On Billing Window: 72 Hours
- **description:** The soft add-on customer is billed exactly 72 hours (3 days) after opting in, and only if they are "ecstatic" with the product. This three-day window aligns with the email sequence designed to drive consumption before the billing event.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 3 and pdf (Lesson 3)
- **domains:** offer-design, pricing, email-marketing

---

**HEUR-TB-104**
- **name:** Soft Add-On Conversion Rate: 30â€“35%+
- **description:** A well-structured soft add-on should convert at 30â€“35% or more. This is the expected benchmark for a pay-nothing-now proposition that is congruent and adds value to the customer captivation offer.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 3 (mp4)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, marketing-funnels, performance-benchmarks

---

**HEUR-TB-105**
- **name:** Mid-Tier Add-On Priced Between $297 and $497
- **description:** The mid-tier add-on is the traditional upsell and should be priced between $297 and $497, always offered with a three-pay option. By this point in the sequence, the customer has proven themselves as an engaged buyer, making this price point achievable.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 4 and pdf (Lesson 4)
- **domains:** pricing, offer-design, marketing-funnels

---

**HEUR-TB-106**
- **name:** Three-Pay Option: Total Slightly Higher than Single Pay
- **description:** When offering a three-pay option on a $297 offer, price it at approximately $109/payment (total ~$327, ~10% more than the single pay). A significant number of buyers will choose the three-pay even though it costs more total, making it a net revenue increase with no loss of single-pay conversions.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 4 (mp4)
- **domains:** pricing, offer-design, revenue-optimization

---

**HEUR-TB-107**
- **name:** Thank You Page Gift Offer Priced Up to $27â€“$47/Month
- **description:** The continuity offer presented as a thank-you gift on the mock thank-you page should be priced between $27 and $47 per month. It is presented as a free 30-day trial, framed as a gift, with full disclosure that billing occurs at end of trial if not canceled.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 6 and pdf (Lesson 6)
- **domains:** pricing, offer-design, continuity-programs

---

**HEUR-TB-108**
- **name:** Prospect Follow-Up Sequence: 7 Emails Over 7 Days
- **description:** The prospect follow-up sequence (List 0) runs for exactly 7 days with one email per day. Day 1: Hook/Promise; Day 2: Transformation Story #1; Day 3: PAS; Day 4: Big Benefit #1; Day 5: Big Benefit #2; Day 6: Transformation Story #2; Day 7: Frankly Puzzled.

- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design, performance-benchmarks

---

**HEUR-TB-109**
- **name:** Main Customer Thank You Sequence: 9 Emails Over 10 Days
- **description:** List C (main customer thank-you sequence) runs for 10 days: Day 1 Welcome; Days 2/4/6 Consumption; Day 7 Bonus Webinar Notice; Days 8/9 Bonus Webinar Reminders; Day 10 Webinar. Consumption emails skip days to avoid fatigue while building anticipation.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design, customer-onboarding

---

**HEUR-TB-110**
- **name:** Add-On Second-Chance Sequences: 3 Emails Over 3 Days
- **description:** Each of the second-chance sequences (Lists 1, 2, 4) contains exactly 3 emails: Day 1 access to purchase, Day 2 second-chance offer, Day 3 reminder. These are intentionally short mini-sequences, not prolonged campaigns.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design

---

**HEUR-TB-111**
- **name:** Soft Add-On Sequence: 4 Emails Over 4 Days (Consumption-First)
- **description:** The soft add-on sequence (List 3) has 4 emails: Day 1 access, Day 2 consumption, Day 3 consumption + second-chance for mid-tier, Day 4 reminder. The extra consumption days align with the 72-hour billing window â€” drive engagement before charging.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design, offer-design

---

**HEUR-TB-112**
- **name:** Premium Offer Sequence: 8 Emails Over 8 Days with Case Studies
- **description:** The premium offer thank-you sequence (List 6) spans 8 days: Day 1 access, Days 2/3/6 consumption, Day 4 customer case study #1, Day 8 customer case study #2. Case studies (not transformation stories) are specifically used for premium buyers to drive consumption of the high-ticket product.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design, premium-products

---

**HEUR-TB-113**
- **name:** Order Form: 5 Essential Elements
- **description:** Every order form needs exactly 5 elements: (1) Affirmation copy in the prospect's voice restating the main benefit and recapping the offer, (2) Recap of the offer, (3) Product-specific testimonials overcoming common objections, (4) Guarantee seal, (5) Security seals. Missing any element reduces conversion.
- **source_file:** Module 5 - Lesson 1 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, copywriting, order-forms

---

**HEUR-TB-114**
- **name:** Transaction Maximization Pages: "10 Minutes" Email Delivery Notice
- **description:** On the first transaction maximization page (reverse add-on), always include a notice that product details will be delivered via email in 10 minutes. This prevents customers from abandoning the sequence to check their inbox and gives them permission to stay and consume the next offer.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 5 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, funnel-design, consumer-psychology

**HEUR-TB-115**
- **name:** Mid-Tier Add-On: Use Delayed Add-to-Cart Button (VSL)
- **description:** Higher-priced mid-tier offers ($297â€“$497) require a delayed add-to-cart button when presented as a VSL, because the price point demands more marketing, more FABs (features, advantages, benefits), and more objection handling before showing the price. Soft and reverse add-ons use immediate buttons due to their low-friction nature.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 4 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, offer-design, copywriting

## L4 â€” FRAMEWORKS (FW-TB-NNN)

---

**FW-TB-100**
- **name:** Ideal Order Form Layout Framework (5-Element Grid)
- **description:** The winning order form layout places affirmation copy + offer recap at top-left, security seals at top-right, testimonials in blocks on the right column, payment input + submit button in the center-bottom, and guarantee seal at bottom-right. This layout has been tested and is the proven winner â€” but should still be tested per market.
- **source_file:** Module 5 - Lesson 1 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, order-forms, funnel-design

---

**FW-TB-101**
- **name:** Transaction Maximization Sequence (TMS) Framework
- **description:** A four-offer sequence designed to maximize front-end transaction value: (1) Reverse Add-On â€” priced â‰¤80% of CCO, immediate button, VSL or short-form; (2) Soft Add-On â€” $67â€“$97, pay-nothing-now, 72-hr billing, immediate button; (3) Mid-Tier Add-On â€” $297â€“$497 with 3-pay, VSL with delayed button; (4) Thank You Gift Offer â€” $27â€“$47/mo continuity, mock thank-you page, no button delay, framed as gift.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lessons 1â€“6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** marketing-funnels, offer-design, revenue-optimization

---

**FW-TB-102**
- **name:** Transaction Maximization Page Elements Framework (6 Elements)
- **description:** Every TMS page (excluding thank-you gift page) must contain: (1) Continuity of branding; (2) Conveys transaction is still in progress; (3) Acknowledges continuance of order; (4) Displays checkout progress status bar; (5) "Product details sent in 10 minutes" notice; (6) Highlights special/exclusive offer. The thank-you gift page deliberately breaks all of these to reset psychology.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 5 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** conversion-optimization, funnel-design, copywriting

---

**FW-TB-103**
- **name:** Amplification Autoresponder Chain â€” 8-Sequence Architecture
- **description:** The complete follow-up system consists of 8 behavior-triggered lists: List 0 (prospect, 7 days), List C (customer thank-you + webinar drive, 10 days), List 1 (CCO second-chance for reverse add-on, 3 emails), List 2 (reverse add-on thank-you + SC for soft, 3 emails), List 3 (soft add-on + SC for mid-tier, 4 emails), List 4 (mid-tier + SC for TYP gift, 3 emails), List 5 (TYP gift access, 2 emails), List 6 (premium offer thank-you, 8 emails).
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lessons 1, 3, 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, marketing-automation, marketing-funnels

---

**FW-TB-104**
- **name:** Chain Automation Logic Framework (Subscribe/Unsubscribe Rules)
- **description:** SamCart handles all subscribe actions (puts buyer on next list). Aweber handles all unsubscribe actions (removes buyer from prior lists). The rule: when someone is subscribed to List N, unsubscribe from all Lists 0 through N-1. This ensures no customer receives redundant second-chance offers for items they've already purchased.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 5 (mp4)
- **domains:** email-marketing, marketing-automation, technical-implementation

---

**FW-TB-105**
- **name:** Prospect Follow-Up Email Sequence (7-Day Framework)
- **description:** 7-email sequence for non-buying optins: Day 1 hook/promise (watch video), Day 2 customer transformation story #1, Day 3 problem/agitate/solve, Day 4 big benefit #1, Day 5 big benefit #2, Day 6 customer transformation story #2, Day 7 "Frankly I'm Puzzled" objection overcome. Each email drives back to the CCO video.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** email-marketing, sequence-design, copywriting

---

**FW-TB-106**
- **name:** Affirmation Copy Framework for Order Forms
- **description:** Affirmation copy must: (1) be written entirely in the prospect's voice ("Yes, I want..."); (2) open with the main benefit/promise that the prospect desires most; (3) recap the specific offer components; (4) restate the outcome/transformation they will achieve. Examples: Gary Halbert's "Yes, I dearly want to attend your three-and-a-half-day master's summit" model.
- **source_file:** Module 5 - Lesson 1 (mp4)
- **domains:** copywriting, order-forms, conversion-optimization

---

**FW-TB-107**
- **name:** Transformation Story Framework (3-Phase Structure)
- **description:** Phase 1 â€” Character starts in same or worse situation than the prospect (amplifies "if they can do it, so can I" effect). Phase 2 â€” Discovery: finding your solution after searching and failing. Phase 3 â€” Transformation: the life the prospect wants to live. Can use real customer, relevant third-party story, or clearly-labeled fictional tale. Works because it transfers the message via narrative rather than explicit claims.
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** copywriting, storytelling, email-marketing

---

**FW-TB-108**
- **name:** Digital Product Fulfillment Options Framework (3-Tier)
- **description:** Three fulfillment methods in ascending order of security and investment: (1) Password-protected WordPress page â€” free, instant, but password can be shared; start here when launching; (2) Encrypted download â€” limits time window, decreases piracy, ~$20/month if not built into cart; (3) Membership site plugin (e.g. Wishlist Member) â€” full IP restriction, per-user passwords, most secure, ~$300 + learning curve.
- **source_file:** Module 8 - Lesson 1 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** product-fulfillment, info-products, technical-implementation

## L5 â€” METHODOLOGIES (MET-TB-NNN)

---

**MET-TB-100**
- **name:** Transaction Maximization Sequence â€” Complete Build Process
- **description:** Step-by-step process: (1) Build customer captivation offer in primary platform (e.g. LeadPages). (2) Create all 4 TMS offer pages inside SamCart as one-click add-ons (no re-entry of payment details). (3) Duplicate all 4 TMS pages externally (e.g. in LeadPages) for second-chance use. (4) Set up 8 lists in Aweber matching all 8 sequences. (5) Configure SamCart to subscribe buyers to correct Aweber list on each purchase. (6) Configure Aweber automation rules to unsubscribe from prior lists when subscribed to next. (7) Write and schedule all emails per sequence framework. (8) Host bonus webinar at Day 10 of List C (evergreen or live).
- **source_file:** Module 7 - Lessons 3â€“6 (mp4)
- **domains:** marketing-funnels, technical-implementation, marketing-automation

---

**MET-TB-101**
- **name:** Soft Add-On 72-Hour Trial Offer â€” Implementation Process
- **description:** Step-by-step: (1) Create product page presenting offer as "pay nothing now â€” get instant access." (2) Configure billing trigger at 72 hours via CRM/SamCart. (3) Write page copy acknowledging customer hasn't yet experienced the initial product and this is a trust-building gesture. (4) Email Day 1: access + consumption teaser. Day 2: consumption motivation. Day 3 (billing day): consumption + second-chance for next offer. Day 4: reminder. (5) Include explicit billing disclosure â€” customer will be charged if they don't cancel before 72 hours. Present with full transparency while keeping gift/trust framing.
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 3 + Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4)
- **domains:** offer-design, email-marketing, technical-implementation

---

**MET-TB-102**
- **name:** Thank You Page Gift Offer â€” Implementation Process
- **description:** Step-by-step: (1) Build a page that looks and feels like a standard thank-you page â€” include thank-you statement at top, do NOT use TMS branding/progress bar. (2) Auto-play VSL that thanks customer for purchase and presents "free gift." (3) Frame offer as a complimentary month of continuity (membership/newsletter), not a trial. (4) CTA button says "Yes, I'll Take My Free Month" (not "Add to Cart"). (5) Disclose billing: at end of the free month, charge $27â€“$47/month unless canceled. (6) Set up in SamCart or CRM as 30-day trial + recurring billing. (7) List 5 in Aweber: Day 1 access email, Day 2 follow-up ("Were you about to access your free gift?").
- **source_file:** Module 6 - Lesson 6 + Module 7 - Lesson 6 (mp4 + pdf)
- **domains:** offer-design, continuity-programs, technical-implementation

---

**MET-TB-103**

**MM-TB-001**

**MM-TB-003**

- **description:** In direct response, the three success determinants in order of importance are: (1) List/Audience, (2) Offer, (3) Copy. A great list with a weak offer outperforms a great copy with the wrong list.
- **source_file:** Module 1/transcription_mp4_module1-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** direct-response, marketing-funnels, conversion

**MM-TB-004**
- **name:** Front End vs Back End Marketing Lens

- **description:** Marketing (talking about the prospect's world) must precede selling (introducing the product). Jumping straight to selling without marketing first kills conversions. Marketing creates desire; selling channels it.

**MM-TB-006**
- **name:** White Space / Gap Analysis in Competitor Messaging
- **description:** By mapping what competitors are already saying to the market, a marketer can identify what is NOT being said â€” the "white space" â€” and construct a hook that occupies unexplored territory.
- **source_file:** Module 1/transcription_mp4_module1-lesson4.txt
- **domains:** market-research, competitive-analysis, messaging

**MM-TB-007**
- **name:** Two-Step Marketing Model
- **description:** The right two-step process gets prospects to first identify themselves as interested (raise their hand), then delivers a lead magnet that simultaneously provides value AND does the marketing/selling â€” not separate activities.
- **source_file:** Module 3/transcription_mp4_module3-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** lead-generation, marketing-funnels, two-step-marketing

**MM-TB-008**
- **name:** Education-Based Marketing (EBM) Content

- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** content-marketing, copywriting, conversion

**MM-TB-009**
- **name:** Niche Viability Framework (John Carleton's Mob)
- **description:** A viable niche must simultaneously be: Large, Hungry, a Mob (congregating), Addicts (insatiable), Flush with Cash, and Accessible. All six criteria must be present for a niche to support a major business.
- **source_file:** Module 1/transcription_mp4_module1-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** niche-selection, market-research, business-model

**MM-TB-010**
- **name:** Pre-Selling as Seed-Planting
- **description:** Front-end products can plant desire seeds for back-end offers through nonchalant references, incomplete information, and client result mentions â€” without overt selling. This mental model separates product creation from embedded marketing.

- **domains:** product-creation, pre-selling, marketing-funnels

**MM-TB-011**

## L3 â€” HEURISTICS (Practical Rules, Thresholds, Decision Shortcuts)

**HEUR-TB-001**
- **name:** Minimum 50,000 Active Prospects for Tight Niches
- **description:** For ultra-tight targeted niches (e.g., dentists, chiropractors), the minimum viable niche size is 50,000 active prospects. Below this threshold, building a six- or seven-figure business becomes extremely difficult.

**HEUR-TB-002**
- **name:** Customer Captivation Offer Priced $19â€“$29
- **description:** The front-end Customer Captivation Offer should be priced between $19 and $29 â€” low enough as a barrier-of-entry but high enough to filter out low-quality buyers. Avoid $7â€“$12 "junk" price points.
- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson1.txt

**HEUR-TB-003**
- **name:** Email Only on Opt-In Form (No First Name)
- **description:** Asking only for email (not first name + email) consistently produces higher opt-in rates. The perceived value of personalization does not outweigh the conversion rate cost of adding an extra form field.

**HEUR-TB-004**
- **name:** Competitors Advertising = Proof Prospects Are Buyers
- **description:** The presence of competitors consistently advertising similar products to a niche is a positive signal â€” it confirms that the market buys. Absence of competitors means the market likely does not convert.
- **source_file:** Module 1/transcription_mp4_module1-lesson1.txt
- **domains:** niche-selection, market-research, competitive-analysis

**HEUR-TB-005**
- **name:** Most Markets Fall at Stage 3â€“4 Sophistication
- **description:** The majority of markets operate at sophistication stage 3 (mechanism-based) or stage 4 (unique mechanism + expanded claim). Messaging targeting stage 1 or 2 in these markets will fail.
- **source_file:** Module 1/transcription_mp4_module1-lesson2.txt
- **domains:** copywriting, market-research, messaging

**HEUR-TB-006**
- **name:** Every 6thâ€“8th Slide Should Break Pattern in VSL
- **description:** In an "ugly VSL," every 6th to 8th slide should not match the script word-for-word. This creates a visual pattern interrupt that re-engages viewers who may have started passively listening.
- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** video-sales-letters, conversion, content-production

**HEUR-TB-007**
- **name:** More Slides, Less Text per Slide in VSL
- **description:** VSL slides should have one sentence or less per slide, with constant movement. More slides with less text creates engagement; fewer slides with dense text causes drop-off.
- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** video-sales-letters, content-production, conversion

**HEUR-TB-008**
- **name:** No Add-to-Cart Button on Page Load
- **description:** The add-to-cart button on the Customer Captivation Offer page should be hidden on arrival and only appear when the offer is revealed in the VSL. Immediate visibility of the button raises sales resistance.
- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** conversion-rate-optimization, offer-creation, video-sales-letters

**HEUR-TB-009**
- **name:** No Header, No Headline, No Branding on VSL Page
- **description:** The Customer Captivation page should have no header graphic, no headline, and no visible add-to-cart button on load. Testing shows that adding a headline consistently suppresses response.
- **source_file:** Module 4/transcription_mp4_module4-lesson3.txt
- **domains:** conversion-rate-optimization, landing-pages, video-sales-letters

**HEUR-TB-010**
- **name:** Go Through Training Twice Before Acting
- **description:** The recommended consumption method is to go through the full training once for overview context, then a second time to execute the action steps module by module before proceeding to the next.
- **source_file:** Introduction/transcription_mp4_Introduction___Six-Figure_Funnel_Formula.txt
- **domains:** education, learning, systems-thinking

**HEUR-TB-011**
- **name:** Pre-Headline Calls Out Audience, Not Benefit
- **description:** The pre-headline on a lead magnet page is an audience identifier ("For people 54 and older..."), not a benefit statement. Its function is to make the right prospect self-select in and others self-select out.

- **domains:** lead-generation, opt-in-pages, copywriting

**HEUR-TB-012**

**HEUR-TB-013**

- **description:** Premiums/bonuses in a Customer Captivation Offer must be high-value enough that a prospect would purchase the bonus alone if offered separately. Throwaway bonuses reduce rather than increase perceived value.

- **domains:** offer-creation, customer-acquisition, conversion

**HEUR-TB-014**
- **name:** Front-End Goal Is Break Even on Ad Spend
- **description:** The correct benchmark for front-end marketing performance is breaking even â€” recovering the advertising cost while acquiring new customers. Expecting front-end profit is a strategic mistake.

- **domains:** paid-traffic, customer-acquisition, business-model

- **description:** Niches with regulatory or legality constraints (medical, supplements, finance) require additional compliance infrastructure and add complexity to marketing. Staying away from these is recommended for simplicity.

**FW-TB-001**

Stage 4: Unique mechanism + expanded claim (pharmaceutical grade + "20 lbs in 20 days"). Stage 5: Prospect identification ("Tired of taking supplements and not losing weight?").

The most powerful funnels are combinations of multiple map types â€” a straight-to-VSL funnel can absorb webinar elements, a squeeze-page funnel can add post-purchase back-end webinar layers, etc. Skilled marketers see the 17 maps as a toolkit to combine, not as isolated templates.

- **Domains:** marketing-funnels, funnel-architecture, direct-response

**MM-TB-306**
- **Name:** Consumption-Based Sequence Progression
- **Description:** In multi-video sequences, prospects should advance based on actual behavior (clicks, consumption) â€” not on arbitrary time delays. If someone clicked to watch Video 2, they get the Video 3 sequence the next day. If they didn't click, they stay on the Video 2 sequence. Engaged prospects move faster; disengaged prospects get more touches before advancing.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #1 Next Level Marketing Funnel Maps/transcription_mp4_NLMFM-Training.txt`
- **Domains:** email-marketing, segmentation, funnel-architecture

---

**MM-TB-307**
- **Name:** The Offer Is More Important Than the Copy
- **Description:** In the hierarchy of direct response marketing, (1) the list is most important, (2) the offer is second, (3) the copy is third. Great copy never makes up for a weak offer. But a killer offer can compensate for weak copy. Most marketers misallocate attention â€” they obsess over copy while treating the offer as an afterthought.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-September2013.txt`
- **Domains:** copywriting, offer-construction, direct-response

---

**MM-TB-308**
- **Name:** Product Introduction Engineering (Anti-Delay Protocol)
- **Description:** Most info-product customers never consume, partially consume, or delay starting. The product introduction must: (1) Resell the purchase decision, (2) Reignite excitement about benefits, (3) Give a clear consumption plan, (4) Deliver a fast win within 24-72 hours. The biggest obstacle is getting started â€” anything that accelerates the first step feeds ongoing consumption.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-November2013.txt`
- **Domains:** product-creation, customer-onboarding, info-marketing

---

**MM-TB-309**
- **Name:** The Big Idea as Funnel Foundation
- **Description:** Every wildly successful marketing funnel is built off a big idea. A big idea cuts through market clutter, conveys newness, makes an almost unbelievable promise, sparks curiosity, and prevents "I've seen this before" mental opt-out. With sophisticated markets (Gene Schwartz), big ideas are the drivers behind the highest-converting funnels â€” not just clever copy or strong offers alone.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-September2013.txt`
- **Domains:** copywriting, marketing-funnels, offer-construction

**MM-TB-310**
- **Name:** Product Pipeline Preview (Upsell Seeding)
- **Description:** Throughout a front-end product, subtly reference what the advanced tribe members are learning and doing. This subconsciously sells new customers on what the next step is. Done 2-3 times throughout a product, it creates both rational desire (they know more) and emotional/psychological desire (fear of missing out on deeper content). The product becomes its own upsell vehicle.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-November2013.txt`

## L3 â€” HEURISTICS (HEUR-TB-300+)

---

**HEUR-TB-300**
- **Name:** Follow Up 10-20 Times Before Giving Up
- **Description:** The "big boys" of direct response marketing make 10-20 contacts with a prospect before giving up on a particular offer. Most small marketers give up after 1-3 follow-ups. Tolerance for sustained follow-up is a core competitive advantage â€” you must be willing to lose the relationship to get it right.

**HEUR-TB-301**
- **Name:** 3:1 Content-to-Pitch Ratio for House List
- **Description:** For the house list of prospects, maintain a 3:1 ratio of content to pitch emails. Three pieces of content (which can contain soft offers) for every one pure pitch email. This ratio builds goodwill and maintains list health while preserving the ability to make direct commercial offers.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-January2014.txt`

**HEUR-TB-302**
- **Name:** 7-12% Additional Opt-In Recovery via Secondary Squeeze Page
- **Description:** When traffic doesn't opt in to the primary squeeze page and attempts to leave, redirect them to a second squeeze page with a totally different offer, benefit, and premise. This exit-redirect approach recovers an additional 7-12% of traffic as opt-ins, capturing leads that would otherwise be permanently lost.

**HEUR-TB-303**
- **Name:** OTO Countdown Timer: 10 Minutes
- **Description:** One-time offers on thank-you pages should use a 10-minute countdown timer with true enforcement (PageExpirationRobotPro or similar). Tell prospects that whatever they opted in for takes ~10 minutes to arrive in email â€” this keeps them on the OTO page. At timer expiry, redirect to a basic thank-you page; the OTO becomes inaccessible via cookie.

**HEUR-TB-304**
- **Name:** Repurpose Content at Minimum 6 Times
- **Description:** A-player info-marketers repurpose every piece of content at least 6 times across different formats (written, audio, video, webinar, slide deck, social media, etc.). This mindset â€” "how can I use this 6 different ways" â€” is how they create more with less effort and dominate across multiple distribution channels.

- **Description:** Email follow-up sequences at each stage of a funnel (after opt-in, after VSL, after webinar, per video in a multi-video sequence) should run 3-7 days. Per video in a consumption-based sequence: typically 3 emails per video before auto-advancing the prospect to the next segment.

- **Description:** Give new customers a perceived quick win within 24-72 hours of purchase. This doesn't need to be a true victory â€” just a perception of accomplishment. Options: Fast Start Video, Fast Start webinar, customer analysis survey, strategy session scheduling, or critique submission. The goal is to break the inertia of getting started.

- **Description:** DVD/CD physical free-plus-shipping offers can be fulfilled on demand (no minimums) via DiscDelivered.com for approximately $5 per unit including duplication, packaging, and postage. This allows testing physical free offers quickly. A $5-7 S&H charge covers the entire fulfillment cost and captures a credit card for subsequent one-click upsells.

**HEUR-TB-308**
- **Name:** Prepay Discount Conversions: 40% per month, 60% per year
- **Description:** When presenting prepay options alongside a trial offer, a monthly prepay discount drives ~40% savings vs. trial rate; an annual prepay drives ~60% savings. Through testing (Mike and Andy example), a significant percentage of prospects choose prepay options when presented clearly on the order form alongside the trial option.

**HEUR-TB-309**
- **Name:** Survey Your List 6 Times Per Year Per Segment
- **Description:** Routinely survey the house list: one month survey prospects, the next month survey customers. This produces 6 surveys per segment per year. Surveys of 3-5 questions uncover needs, desires, preferred formats, and topics â€” enabling tailored offers built directly from stated demand.

**HEUR-TB-310**
- **Name:** Second Chance Follow-Up Sequence: 2 Days Post-Purchase
- **Description:** After a customer takes a trial or front-end offer but declines an upsell, wait 2 days (giving them time to experience the value of what they bought), then send a second-chance sequence for the declined upsell. Many customers will convert on this second opportunity after tasting the quality of the initial product.

**HEUR-TB-311**

- **Description:** When prospects watch a VSL and exit without buying, redirect them via exit redirect to a long-form sales letter version. The long-form version is 90%+ the word-for-word VSL script with subheads and minimal graphical elements added. This requires always writing a full script for VSLs (never record on the fly).

- **Description:** Before rolling out a new product to the house list, send 2-3 broadcast emails building anticipation, curiosity, and excitement. **FW-TB-300**
- **Name:** The Minimum Viable Funnel (MVF) Framework
- **Description:** An MVF is a basic funnel with just enough to test with paid traffic: squeeze page + sales vehicle (VSL or long-form letter) + solid offer. It does NOT include a built-out upsell/downsell sequence or advanced conversion elements. Purpose: prove that the big idea resonates with the market AND that the offer converts. Only after MVF validation does the marketer build out optimization elements.

**FW-TB-301**
- **Name:** The Irresistible Offer Construction Framework
- **Description:** To build a truly irresistible offer, combine: (1) "Try before you buy" proposition (soft/trial offer), (2) Lopsided value proposition (deliverables worth far more than price), (3) High-value product-enhancing premiums (free bonuses), (4) Long-timeframe money-back guarantee, (5) Urgent reason to respond now with a credible "reason why." This combination eliminates risk, conveys control, and makes competitors' offers look ridiculous.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Letter-September2013.txt`

Each map is modular and can be combined with others.

**MET-TB-308**
- **Name:** The MFA Ultra-Stripped Squeeze Page + VSL Control Method
- **Description:** Todd Brown's own tested MVF: (1) Minimal squeeze page â€” no images, no video, simple headline + opt-in box (this was the control over "improved" versions), (2) Negative exclusion pre-head + curiosity headline, (3) VSL functions as its own lead magnet for low-price offers (no separate lead magnet needed), (4) Delayed CTA tested: 20-60 minute delay depending on price point â€” higher price = longer delay, (5) Serial position effect for testimonials and bullets (most powerful first AND last), (6) Upsell rule exception: 60%+ conversion with no VSL on upsell, similar price to front-end, (7) Payment plan beats single pay ($3Ã—$87 > $199 single payment), (8) Second Chance offer via email follow-up 2 days post-purchase for declined upsell.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #2 Marketing Funnels Uncensored/MFU Archive/transcription_pdf_MFU-Blueprint-Report-4.txt`
- **Domains:** funnel-architecture, conversion-optimization, copywriting

---

**MET-TB-309**
- **Name:** The Product Type Selection Method
- **Description:** Select product type based on goal and market stage: Written (PDFs, reports) â€” fast to create, easy to consume, works for entry-level; Audio (recordings, podcasts) â€” intimacy and convenience; Video (screen capture, talking head, live events) â€” highest perceived value, most flexibility; Continuity (newsletter, membership) â€” recurring revenue, relationship-building; Compilation (interviews, aggregated content) â€” leverages external authority, fast to produce. Choose continuity for backend LTV; choose written/video for front-end. Mix formats across the product line for maximum repurposing efficiency.
- **Source_file:** `Engineering Profitable Products/transcription_pdf_EPP-Training-Slides.txt`
- **Domains:** product-creation, info-marketing, business-model

---

**MET-TB-310**
- **Name:** The Two-Step Marketing Implementation Method
- **Description:** Never send cold traffic directly to a sales page for high-priced offers. Step 1: Capture opt-in via lead magnet, free report, or VSL as lead magnet (for lower prices). Step 2: Sell via email follow-up sequence. The follow-up sequence does the majority of conversions. Exception: "Ultra-strong copy + offer" straight-to-VSL works for expert copywriters (tested). For most marketers, two-step outperforms one-step by capturing a significant percentage of conversions from follow-up that would otherwise be lost.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #1 Next Level Marketing Funnel Maps/transcription_mp4_NLMFM-Training.txt`
- **Domains:** funnel-architecture, lead-generation, email-marketing

---

**MET-TB-311**
- **Name:** The Cart Abandonment Retargeting Recovery Method
- **Description:** (1) Drop retargeting pixel on order form page load (not purchase confirmation), (2) Prospects who land on order form but don't complete = cart abandoners, (3) Email sequence: "I see you started your order but didn't get a chance to complete it â€” here's the link," optionally linking to a different order form with a reassurance video at the top, (4) Retargeting ads drive same prospects back to order form, (5) After purchase: burn pixel in first upsell page removes buyer from retargeting and email abandonment sequence. Captures significant percentage of sales that most marketers permanently lose.
- **Source_file:** `Upsell #1 Next Level Marketing Funnel Maps/transcription_mp4_NLMFM-Training.txt`
- **Domains:** conversion-optimization, email-marketing, paid-traffic

---

## PERFIL

**Nome:** Todd Brown
**Expertise:** Marketing Funnels, Direct Response, Customer Acquisition
**Slug:** todd-brown
**Material Processado:** Six Figure Funnel Formula (12 modules) + 2 Upsells + 3 Bonuses

---

## RESUMO EXECUTIVO

Todd Brown is a direct response marketer specializing in customer acquisition funnels. His signature system â€” the Six Figure Funnel Formula (6FFF) â€” is a complete funnel architecture covering the entire customer journey from lead magnet to backend marketing.

**Diferenciadores Chave:**
- Complete funnel architecture (not just one piece)
- Heavy emphasis on front-end as INVESTMENT, not profit center
- Transaction Maximization Sequence (4 structured upsell types with exact pricing)
- 8-Sequence Amplification Autoresponder Chain (behavior-driven)
- 17 Funnel Map taxonomy (modular, combinable)
- Strong metrics framework (5 Essential Metrics, 6-Scenario Decision Matrix)
- Adapted Eugene Schwartz's Marketplace Sophistication for modern funnel builders

---

## FILOSOFIA CENTRAL

1. **Front-end = Customer Acquisition Investment** â€” Never try to profit on the front end. Break even, build customer base, profit on backend.
2. **The marketer who can spend the most wins** â€” TMS exists to raise the acquisition ceiling.
3. **List > Offer > Copy** â€” Priority hierarchy for direct response success.
4. **Specificity sells, vagueness kills** â€” Every claim needs specific numbers and proof.
5. **Data drives decisions** â€” No assumptions, only testing data.
6. **Consumption drives repeat purchases** â€” If they don't consume, they won't buy again.
7. **You cannot make enough offers to customers*

## APRENDIZADOS â€” A-Z COPYWRITING WORKSHOP (105 novos elementos)

### Copywriting Craft & Writing

| # | Insight | Layer | ID | Source |
|---|---------|-------|----|--------|
| 1 | Good copy is verb-driven, not adjective-driven. Verbs = movement, action, transformation. | L1 | FIL-TB-040 | AZCW |
| 2 | Copy must sound like spoken word. Simple/Short/Short. Flesch-Kincaid < 8. | L1 | FIL-TB-041 | AZCW |
| 3 | Replace adverbs with single strong verbs: slowly walk â†’ strut, gently touch â†’ caress | L3 | HEUR-TB-062 | AZCW |
| 4 | Use emotionally-charged words: embarrass â†’ humiliate, tired â†’ exhausted, broke â†’ crushed | L3 | HEUR-TB-067 | AZCW |
| 5 | Don't emote, evoke! "You'll be excited when you hear" not "I'm so excited to tell you" | L1 | FIL-TB-044 | AZCW |
| 6 | Replace "if" with "when" (presupposition). "But" negates prior statement â€” use "and" instead. | L3 | HEUR-TB-064,066 | AZCW |

### Hooks, Themes & Ideas

| # | Insight | Layer | ID | Source |
|---|---------|-------|----|--------|
| 1 | Categorical Imperative (Bonner/Agora): prevent prospect from categorizing idea into known bucket | L2 | MM-TB-045 | AZCW |

---

## Comprehensive DNA Extraction Summary: Todd Brown -- A-Z Copywriting Workshop, Day 2, Part 1

### SOURCE METADATA
- **Speaker:** Todd Brown
- **Material:** A-Z Copywriting Workshop, Day 2, Part 1
- **File:** `transcription_mp4_03-A_to_Z_Copywriting_-__Day_2_-_Pt__1.txt`
- **Size:** ~37KB
- **Context:** Live workshop (Day 2 of multi-day training), covering foundational principles of hooks, themes, and ideas in copywriting/marketing

---

### PHILOSOPHIES (L1)

1. **"We're Really in the Idea Business"** -- Attributed to Mark Ford / Michael Masterson (founder of Agora). Todd says Mark told him: "We realized early on that we're really in the idea business. We're in the business of creating, developing, disseminating, interesting, compelling, contrarian ideas." Todd considers this the foundational philosophy of all direct response marketing.

2. **Different Over More of the Same** -- Todd's core operating philosophy: "I am always looking for different. My team hears me say all the time, different, different. I don't want more of the same because more of the same doesn't garner attention."

3. **People Are Interested in New and Different** -- "People aren't looking for more of the same. They're looking for new, they're looking for different. They associate new and different with better."

4. **Narrow Over Wide** -- "You are better off narrowing what you're talking about and getting more specific, rather than going wide, rather than opening up with more ideas, rather than trying to appeal to more and more people with a broader message. The wider you go, the more vague, the more general you get, the less you appeal to anybody."

5. **A Confused Prospect Doesn't Buy** -- "A confused prospect doesn't remain engaged. A confused prospect doesn't buy. It has to be easily understandable, immediately understandable."

6. **Benefits Can Never Be Too Many** -- "I've never seen a campaign fail because it had too many benefits. I've only seen campaigns fail because they didn't have enough benefits."

7. **Data-Driven Decisions Over Subjective Opinions** -- "All of our decisions should be driven by data or the most amount of decisions possible should be driven objectively, not subjectively."

8. **Front End Is for Maximum Buyer Acquisition** -- "The front end is all about maximum buyer acquisition." Don't over-optimize a campaign that is already hitting KPIs; instead, build back ends to increase lifetime value.

---

### MENTAL MODELS (L2)

1. **The Categorical Imperative (Bill Bonner's Version)** -- Learned from Bill Bonner, founder of Agora. The brain categorizes new information into known buckets as a cognitive shortcut. If a prospect can categorize your message into a known bucket, they mentally opt out. The goal of your hook is to prevent categorization -- to create enough curiosity and mystery that the prospect cannot file it into a known box.

2. **Mental Opt-Out** -- The moment a prospect says "Oh, I've heard that before" or "I know what this is," they check out. The marketer's job is to prevent this mental opt-out by making the message feel genuinely new and uncategorizable.

3. **Emotionally Compelling + Intellectually Interesting** -- Every effective hook must be BOTH: (a) emotionally compelling (from promise + unique mechanism = hope), and (b) intellectually interesting (from a storyline/angle they've never heard = curiosity). Both must be present.

4. **The Extraordinary Way to Present the Ordinary** -- When you can't find something absolutely different, ask: "What is the extraordinary way to talk about the ordinary? What is the unique angle, perspective, or story that nobody else is telling about the common thing?"

5. **Curse of Knowledge** -- The closer you are to your topic (scale: 1-10, you're at 9), the more you assume your prospect knows. This creates inaccuracies in what you think the audience understands. You must fight this by stepping into the prospect's shoes. "The more you know and the longer you are around a topic, the more inaccuracies there are in what you assume your audience knows and understands."

6. **Discovery Feeling** -- The best hooks trigger a feeling of discovery in the prospect: "Oh my God, this is big. This could be the thing. This could be the answer. Holy moly, this could change everything." The message should feel like stumbling onto something newsworthy.

7. **Payoff + Mystery = Engagement** -- Promise and curiosity are the twin engines. Reframed multiple ways: "payoff and mystery," "benefit and intrigue," "I have to know more -- how can that be? Sounds awesome, but what is it?"

---

### HEURISTICS (L3)

1. **Hook Self-Test Questions** (ask BEFORE launching):
   - Will my prospects say "I've heard this before"?
   - Can they categorize it into a known bucket?
   - Will they find it intellectually interesting?
   - Will they say "this is big, this changes everything"?
   - Push yourself until you can answer YES to the last two.

2. **KPI-Based Optimization Heuristic** -- Once a campaign hits your predetermined KPI (e.g., break-even on buyer acquisition), stop optimizing it. Let it run. Scale traffic. "I know what the performance is that I want from a campaign going in. Once I hit it, I'm done." Only make incremental improvements when a campaign falls OUT of KPI to get it back IN.

3. **Don't Over-Test When Already Winning** -- "You can try to optimize the performance of a campaign until you are blue in the face. That is a never-ending process." Instead, if hitting KPIs, invest energy in building back-end offers to increase LTV.

4. **One Hook First, Test Later** -- Start with the hook you believe is best. If it meets KPIs, keep it. Don't split-test prematurely. "Could I potentially test another hook and get it to perform better? Maybe. But if it's already performing within the KPI that I want, I'm going to let it roll."

5. **Break-Even Buyer Acquisition Threshold** -- Example given: Spend $1,000 in Facebook ads, generate $1,000 in revenue = acceptable break-even front-end KPI. Once achieved, scale rather than optimize.

6. **Immediate Understandability Test** -- "Read it, see it, hear it -- I get it." If the prospect needs to read the lead to understand the headline, the headline fails. Hooks must be immediately graspable.

7. **Two Mediocre Ideas Combined = One Weak Idea** -- "Weak marketing messages, weak marketing hooks are crowded with lots of mediocre ideas." When a hook isn't strong enough, marketers bring in additional ideas to bolster it, but this weakens rather than strengthens.

---

### FRAMEWORKS (L4)

1. **The Rule of One** (learned from Michael Masterson / Mark Ford) -- The core structural framework for hooks. Five components:
   - **One Narrative/Storyline** -- A single idea, not multiple storylines
   - **One Main Promise** -- One overarching claim of transformation/result (ancillary benefits come later in the copy, not in the hook)
   - **One Core Emotion** -- Not trying to trigger fear, greed, belonging, FOMO all at once; pick ONE dominant emotion for the hook
   - **One Thing to Think** -- The prospect should walk away thinking ONE thought, not processing multiple ideas
   - This is the antidote to the "multiple mediocre ideas" trap

2. **Hook Power Formula: Promise + Curiosity** -- Two foundational elements of every effective hook:
   - **Promise** = Big, bold, audacious, TRUE, and BELIEVABLE payoff/result/transformation
   - **Curiosity** = Mystery about the mechanism -- "I'm not sure what this is, I want to know more"
   - These create the "I have to know more" response

3. **Hook Checklist** (simple validation framework):
   - Follows the Rule of One
   - Big, believable promise of result
   - Viewed as new, fresh, timely
   - Specific, concrete, tangible (creates mental images in the mind's eye)
   - Intellectually interesting
   - Easy to grasp / immediately understandable

4. **Emotionally Compelling = Promise + Unique Mechanism** -- Emotional power comes from: (a) a big, bold, audacious, true, believable payoff, and (b) a unique mechanism -- a different WAY of achieving that result. Together they create HOPE.

5. **Two-Track Hook Discovery Process**:
   - **Track 1:** Find something absolutely different -- an angle, idea, storyline nobody is talking about
   - **Track 2 (fallback):** If you can't find absolute novelty, find the extraordinary way to present the ordinary -- a different angle on the common thing

---

### METHODOLOGIES (L5)

1. **Hook Development Methodology** -- Todd's personal process for coming up with hooks:
   - **Step 1:** Spend dedicated THINKING TIME -- think through the market, what moves them, what drives them, what they're already seeing and hearing
   - **Step 2:** Look for GAPS -- what is the market not hearing? What aspect of their problem is nobody addressing?
   - **Step 3:** Consume information broadly and ask: "From all the information I'm consuming, what's a different angle? What's an idea nobody is talking about?"
   - **Step 4:** If absolute novelty isn't found, pivot to "What is the extraordinary way to present the ordinary?"
   - **Step 5:** Validate against the Hook Checklist and Rule of One

2. **Campaign Performance Decision Methodology**:
   - **Step 1:** Define your KPI BEFORE launching (e.g., break-even buyer acquisition)
   - **Step 2:** Launch with the hook you believe is best
   - **Step 3:** If campaign meets KPI, leave it running and scale traffic
   - **Step 4:** Only optimize when campaign falls OUT of KPI
   - **Step 5:** If at KPI, invest energy in back-end offers for LTV rather than front-end optimization

---

### KEY QUOTES (Memorable/Signature)

- "We realized early on that we're really in the idea business." -- Mark Ford (as relayed by Todd)
- "Different, different. I don't want more of the same because more of the same doesn't garner attention."
- "What is the extraordinary way to talk about the ordinary?"
- "Weak marketing messages are crowded with lots of mediocre ideas."
- "A confused prospect doesn't remain engaged. A confused prospect doesn't buy."
- "I've never seen a campaign fail because it had too many benefits. I've only seen campaigns fail because they didn't have enough benefits."
- "The wider you go, the more vague, the more general you get, the less you appeal to anybody."
- "Narrow, specific, focus -- one idea, one narrative, one promise, one core emotion, one thing to think."
- "Promise and curiosity -- that's the essence of all effective marketing."
- "Payoff and mystery. Benefit and intrigue."
- "I have to know more. How can that be? Sounds awesome, but what is it?"
- "You can try to optimize the performance of a campaign until you are blue in the face. That is a never-ending process."

---

### INFLUENCES / MENTORS CITED

| Person | Concept Attributed | Relationship |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Bill Bonner** (founder of Agora) | Categorical Imperative -- preventing mental categorization | Learned from early on |
| **Michael Masterson / Mark Ford** (marketing genius behind Agora) | Rule of One; "We're in the idea business" | Personal mentor, drilled into Todd repeatedly |
| **John Ford** & Mark Ford | Co-authors of "Great Leads" -- best book on lead types + Schwartz's awareness levels | Todd calls them buddies |
| **Mark Ford & Will Newman** | "Persuasion: The Subtle Art of Getting What You Want" -- encapsulates the Rule of One | Published ~2014 by AWAI |
| **Eugene Schwartz** | Levels of awareness (referenced indirectly via Great Leads) | Foundational influence |
| **AWAI (American Writers & Artists Inc.)** | Publisher of recommended books; Will Newman associated | Industry connection |

### BOOK RECOMMENDATIONS

1. **"Great Leads"** by Michael Masterson (Mark Ford) & John Ford -- best book on lead types and Eugene Schwartz's levels of awareness
2. **"Persuasion: The Subtle Art of Getting What You Want"** by Mark Ford & Will Newman -- small book, published by AWAI (~2014), "a freaking gem" that encapsulates the Rule of One

---

### DOMAINS COVERED

- Copywriting (hooks, headlines, leads)
- Direct response marketing
- Campaign strategy and optimization
- Buyer acquisition economics (front-end/back-end)
- Marketing psychology (curiosity, mental opt-out, categorization)
- Hook/theme/idea development
